name: LoanMax Title Loans
description_short: Auto title lender founded in 1990 offering loans up to $10,000 using your vehicle title as collateral, with locations across multiple US states.
description_long:
LoanMax Title Loans was founded in 1990 in Jonesboro, Georgia, making it one of the longer-standing title loan companies in the United States. Headquartered in Alpharetta, GA (complaint address: 3440 Preston Ridge Rd., Suite 500), the company operates under the management of Select Management Resources and is licensed in multiple states including Michigan, Virginia, Delaware, South Carolina, New Mexico, and Nevada. Over its 35+ years in business, it has positioned itself as a national provider of vehicle-equity-based lending to consumers who need quick access to cash.

LoanMax offers cash loans on car titles — commonly called auto title loans — of up to $10,000. The application process requires only three items: the borrower's vehicle, a government-issued photo ID, and a clear vehicle title (meaning the vehicle must be owned outright with no liens). Loan approval and amount are subject to vehicle evaluation and ability to repay. Accepted payment methods include cash, check, debit card, and Western Union, and online payments are also available through the customer portal.

What sets LoanMax apart within the title loan segment is its longevity — 35 years of operation — and its multi-state regulatory footprint. Each state operation carries the applicable state license (e.g., Virginia offices operated by Fairfax Elite Financial Services LLC, Delaware licensed by the Delaware State Bank Commissioner). Store hours are consistent across locations: Monday through Friday 10:00AM–6:00PM and Saturday 9:00AM–2:00PM, offering weekend access that many traditional lenders do not provide. The company operates a network of physical branches, with multiple locations clustered in metro areas like greater Detroit.

Honestly assessed, LoanMax is a high-cost lender of last resort. Title loans are explicitly described on the Nevada disclosure as 'short-term financial needs only and not as a long-term financial solution,' and the company itself directs credit-challenged customers to seek credit counseling. The core risk is vehicle repossession: if a borrower cannot repay, they lose the collateral. Interest rates are not disclosed anywhere on the location pages reviewed, which is a significant transparency gap. For consumers who own their vehicle outright, have exhausted lower-cost options, and need $1,000–$10,000 quickly, LoanMax is a legitimate (licensed) option — but the cost and repossession risk are material concerns.
pros: Founded in 1990 — 35+ years of operating history, one of the older title lenders in the US
Loans up to $10,000, higher ceiling than typical payday or emergency cash lenders
Minimal documentation required: vehicle, photo ID, and clear title only
Four payment methods accepted: cash, check, debit card, and Western Union
Online payment portal available for existing borrowers
Saturday hours (9AM–2PM) provide weekend access for borrowers who work weekdays
Licensed and regulated in multiple states with state-specific consumer disclosures provided
cons: Interest rates and APR are not disclosed on location pages — major transparency gap
Vehicle repossession risk if borrower defaults — borrower loses their primary asset
Collateral required: only available to consumers who own their vehicle outright with clear title
Limited hours (closes 6PM weekdays, 2PM Saturday) and in-person-only application process
Nevada and general disclosures explicitly warn this product is not a long-term financial solution
best_for: Vehicle owners who hold a clear title and need $1,000–$10,000 quickly
Borrowers with poor or no credit who cannot qualify for personal loans or credit cards
Consumers who have exhausted payday alternatives and need a larger emergency amount
People in Michigan and other licensed states needing a regulated (not unlicensed) title lender
diagnosis: LoanMax is best suited for vehicle owners with a clear title who need fast access to larger emergency cash amounts ($1,000–$10,000) and cannot qualify for personal loans or credit union alternatives. The primary caveat is significant: title loans put the borrower's vehicle at repossession risk, interest rates are not published on their site, and the company's own state disclosures characterize the product as a short-term-only solution — making it a last resort rather than a first choice.
services: Cash loans on car titles up to $10,000
In-person loan applications at branch locations
Online payment portal for existing borrowers
Multi-state branch network (MI, VA, DE, SC, NM, NV and others)
